kort: A low, safe warm-up: lift your knees alternately at a walking pace and swing your arms along.
steg: Stand tall and lift one knee at a time to hip height or lower.
Swing the opposite arm forward, as if walking.
Keep a steady pace you can talk at.
tips: Pick up the pace or lift your knees higher if you want more heat.

kort: Soft jogging in place — the heels land lightly and your heart rate creeps slowly upward.
steg: Jog in place with short, light steps.
Land softly on the forefoot and let the heels kiss the floor.
Relax your shoulders and breathe calmly.
tips: Switch to marching any time — the warmth comes either way.

kort: Walking at a comfortable pace — used as a warm-up, a rest between intervals and a cool-down.
steg: Walk at a pace where you can talk easily.
Let your arms swing naturally and look ahead.
Use the walk to feel your breathing settle.

kort: Optional light cardio — stair walking, jump-rope motion without a rope, or a brisk march.
steg: Choose a movement that raises your heart rate a little: stairs, marching or small hops.
Keep it light — you should get warm, not tired.
alias: Light cardio (stair walking/hops without a rope)

kort: The classic jumping straddle at an easy pace — arms and legs out and in at the same time.
steg: Jump your feet out to the sides while lifting your arms out and up.
Jump back and lower your arms in one smooth motion.
Land softly with slightly bent knees.
tips: No jumping: step-jacks — step one leg out to the side at a time.

kort: Large, slow circles with straight arms — warms up the shoulders in every direction.
steg: Stretch your arms out to the sides.
Draw large circles forward at an easy pace.
Switch direction halfway.
tips: Start with small circles and let them grow.

kort: Roll your shoulders in large, soft circles — loosens the neck and shoulder area.
steg: Lift your shoulders toward your ears.
Roll them back and down in a smooth circle.
Repeat and switch direction.

kort: Large circles with the hips — like spinning a hula hoop in slow motion.
steg: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and your hands on your hips.
Draw large, slow circles with your hips.
Switch direction halfway.

kort: Slow circles with the ankle — lubricates the joint before walking, running and squats.
steg: Lift one foot slightly off the floor (hold onto something if needed).
Draw slow circles with your toes, both directions.
Switch feet.

kort: Soft circles and tilts with the wrists — prepares you for pressing and support exercises.
steg: Interlace your fingers and draw slow figure-eights with your wrists.
Then tip your hands up and down with straight arms.
Shake them out loosely at the end.

kort: A gentle neck loosener: look to one side, back to center, and over to the other side.
steg: Sit or stand tall and lower your shoulders.
Turn your head calmly to one side and stop where it feels comfortable.
Hold for a couple of breaths and switch sides.
tips: Never force it — the neck likes small, calm movements.

navn: Shoulder rotations with a light dumbbell
kort: External rotation with a light weight — wakes up the rotator cuff before pressing and rowing.
steg: Hold a light dumbbell with your elbow tucked into your side, bent 90 degrees.
Rotate the forearm out to the side, slow and controlled.
Return to start without letting the elbow leave your body.
tips: This should feel light — it's lubrication, not strength training.

navn: Shoulder rotations with an empty bar
kort: Slow rotations and presses with an empty bar — prepares the shoulders for the base lifts.
steg: Hold the bar with a wide grip in front of your body.
Lift it slowly overhead and slightly back, as far as is comfortable.
Return in front of the hips at the same slow pace.

kort: With a straight arm, the band travels from the hips, over the head and behind the back — a full shoulder circle.
steg: Hold the band wide with straight arms in front of your hips.
Bring it slowly up over your head and further back, without bending your elbows.
Come back the same way. Wider grip = easier.
tips: If you feel a pinch in the shoulder: take a wider grip.

kort: In a plank position: push your shoulder blades apart and let them come together — the arms stay straight.
steg: Hold a plank on straight arms.
Without bending your elbows, lower your chest slightly by squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Then push the floor away so your upper back rounds. Repeat slowly.
tips: Small movement — all the work happens between the shoulder blades.

kort: A plank where you lift one hand and tap the opposite shoulder — anti-rotation and shoulder control.
steg: Hold a plank with your feet a little wider than usual.
Lift one hand slowly and place it on the opposite shoulder.
Set it down without your hips twisting. Switch sides.
tips: Imagine a cup of water sitting on your lower back — it must not tip over.

kort: Standing cross-coordination: hand to opposite knee, alternating — wakes up the core and balance.
steg: Stand tall and lift one knee.
Bring the opposite hand down toward the knee.
Switch sides at an easy marching pace.
alias: Standing cross-march, hand to opposite knee

kort: The very foundation of the deadlift: push your hips back with a long spine, hands sliding down the thighs.
steg: Stand with feet hip-width apart and soft knees.
Push your hips straight back while keeping your spine long — hands slide down your thighs.
Stop when you feel a stretch in the back of your thighs, and press your hips forward to standing.
alias: Hip hinge without weight (hands slide down the thighs)
tips: Think "butt toward the wall behind you", not "bend down".

kort: The body's most fundamental strength exercise: sit down between your hips and stand back up.
steg: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out.
Sit down and back calmly, as if toward a chair — heels on the floor.
Go as deep as feels fine, and press back up.
tips: The knees should point the same way as the toes.
Keep your chest proud — that sorts out your back.

kort: A squat with a chair behind you as reassurance — sit almost all the way down, and stand back up.
steg: Stand in front of a chair with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Lower yourself calmly until your seat just touches the chair.
Press back up without using your hands, if you can.
tips: Managing it easily? Try without the chair next time.

kort: Stand up from a chair without your hands, and sit calmly back down — everyday strength at its most important.
steg: Sit on the edge of the chair with your feet under your knees.
Lean forward a little and stand up without pushing off with your hands.
Sit calmly back down — take about 3 seconds down.
alias: Sit-to-stand from a chair (3 s down)
tips: The slower down, the more strength you build.

kort: A squat with weight held to the chest — the weight balances you and makes deep squats easier.
steg: Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell with both hands against your chest.
Sit down calmly between your hips with your heels on the floor.
Press back up with your chest proud.
tips: Your elbows can slide inside your knees at the bottom — that opens the hips.

kort: A deep goblet squat where you stay in the bottom position and rock calmly — opens the hips and ankles.
steg: Hold the weight to your chest and sit all the way down into the bottom of the squat.
Stay there: rock calmly side to side and press your knees out with your elbows.
Stand up after a few calm breaths.
tips: This is mobility with weight — calm, not reps.

kort: The base lift: the bar rests on your upper back while you sit down and stand up with your whole body.
steg: Place the bar on the upper part of your back (not your neck) and lift it out of the rack.
Sit down and back calmly with your chest proud, until your thighs are roughly parallel.
Press your whole foot into the floor and stand up.
tips: Use light weight until the technique is dialed in — depth and control before kilos.
Breathe in at the top, hold your breath on the way down, breathe out on the way up.

kort: Stay seated at the bottom of a deep squat — the hips open up gradually as you breathe.
steg: Sit all the way down into a deep squat with your heels on the floor.
Hold onto something in front of you if needed.
Stay there and breathe calmly — your weight can shift a little side to side.
alias: Deep squat hold with calm weight shifts
Deep squat hold with a rotational reach toward the ceiling
tips: If it pinches in the ankles: place something thin under your heels.

kort: A sideways squat: all your weight over one leg while the other stretches out to the side.
steg: Stand wide and shift your weight over to one side.
Sit down over the bent leg — the other is straight with the toe toward the ceiling.
Come up calmly and switch sides at a slow pace.
tips: Full depth as able — hold onto something for balance if you like.

kort: One long step forward, lower the back knee toward the floor, and push back — strength and balance in one.
steg: Take a good step forward.
Lower the back knee calmly toward the floor — front knee over the foot.
Push off the front foot back to standing, and switch legs.
tips: Take about 3 seconds down — control beats speed.
Wobbling? Shorter step and a fingertip on a wall for support.

kort: Like a lunge, but the step goes backward — gentler on the knees and easier to balance.
steg: Take a controlled step backward.
Lower the back knee toward the floor — the weight stays on the front leg.
Push yourself back to standing and switch sides.
tips: Keep the front heel on the floor the whole way.

kort: A lunge with a dumbbell in each hand — same pattern, more strength.
steg: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Take a step forward and lower the back knee calmly toward the floor.
Push yourself back and switch legs.
tips: Keep your torso tall — the weights should hang quietly.

kort: A backward lunge with dumbbells in your hands — knee-friendly leg strength with extra load.
steg: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and take a calm step backward.
Lower the back knee toward the floor with your torso tall.
Push off the front foot back to standing. Switch sides.

kort: A hip hinge with weight: the dumbbells slide down the thighs as the hips push back — the posterior chain does the work.
steg: Stand with the dumbbells in front of your thighs and soft knees.
Push your hips back with a long spine — the weights slide close along your legs.
Stop when you feel a stretch in the back of your thighs, and press your hips forward to standing.
tips: Keep the spine long the whole way — the movement comes from the hips.
Feel your glutes squeeze at the top.

kort: Lift the bar from the floor to standing with a long spine — the entire posterior chain in one lift.
steg: Stand with your midfoot under the bar, grip just outside your legs.
Lower your hips, lift your chest and brace — your arms are straight.
Press your legs into the floor and extend your hips forward to standing. Lower calmly the same way.
tips: The bar should slide close along your legs the whole way.
Start light and let technique decide the weight.

kort: Lying on your back: press your hips toward the ceiling and squeeze your glutes at the top.
steg: Lie on your back with bent knees and your feet on the floor.
Press through your heels and lift your hips until your body is a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Squeeze your glutes for one second at the top, and lower calmly.
tips: Don't arch your back at the top — it's the glutes that lift.

kort: The classic: lower your chest toward the floor and press up — the body as one stiff plank.
steg: Hold a plank with your hands a little wider than your shoulders.
Lower your chest calmly toward the floor with your elbows about 45 degrees out.
Press back up without losing the plank.
tips: Too easy/hard? Wall → knees → toes is the progression.
The hips follow the body — no sag or peak.

kort: Push-ups with your knees on the floor — same movement, friendlier load.
steg: Get on all fours and walk your hands a little forward, body angled from knees to head.
Lower your chest calmly toward the floor.
Press back up with your body a straight line from the knees.
tips: Brace your abs lightly so the hips don't sink.

kort: Push-ups standing against a wall — the safest way into the pressing movement.
steg: Stand an arm's length from the wall with your hands at shoulder height.
Bend your elbows and let your chest come toward the wall.
Press yourself calmly back.
tips: Farther from the wall = harder.

kort: Press the dumbbells from your chest to straight arms, lying on a bench or the floor.
steg: Lie on a bench (or the floor) with a dumbbell in each hand at your chest.
Press the weights calmly up to straight arms.
Lower under control back to the start.
alias: Light break-in set: dumbbell bench press
tips: Feet firmly on the floor, shoulder blades pulled lightly together.

kort: The base lift for the upper body: lower the bar to your chest and press to straight arms.
steg: Lie with your eyes under the bar, grip a little wider than your shoulders.
Lift out, lower the bar calmly to the middle of your chest.
Press up to straight arms over your shoulders.
tips: Shoulder blades together and down — that gives a stable shelf.
Use a spotter or safety pins on heavier sets.

kort: Press the dumbbells from your shoulders to straight arms overhead, standing.
steg: Stand with your feet hip-width apart, dumbbells at your shoulders.
Brace your abs and press the weights straight up.
Lower calmly back to your shoulders.
tips: Don't lean back — think tall and proud.

kort: The pressing movement without load — a perfect break-in before dumbbells.
steg: Stand tall with your hands at shoulder height.
Press your hands calmly up to straight arms.
Lower under control back down.

kort: With support on a bench: pull the dumbbell toward your hip with the elbow close to the body.
steg: Place a knee and hand on the bench, spine long and parallel to the floor.
Let the dumbbell hang, and pull it calmly up toward your hip crest.
Lower under control. Finish the set and switch sides.
tips: Pull with your back, not your biceps — think elbow toward your back pocket.

kort: Hinged forward with a long spine: pull both dumbbells toward your stomach.
steg: Hip hinge until your torso is well angled, dumbbells hanging.
Pull the weights toward the lower chest/stomach.
Lower calmly — the spine keeps the same angle the whole set.
tips: Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.

kort: Bend your elbows and lift the dumbbells toward your shoulders — controlled up, calm down.
steg: Stand with the dumbbells at your sides, palms forward.
Bend your elbows and lift the weights toward your shoulders without swinging.
Lower calmly all the way down.
tips: The elbows stay at your sides — only the forearms move.

kort: Circle the kettlebell calmly around your head — gentle for the shoulders and upper back.
steg: Hold the bell upside down by the horns in front of your chest.
Circle it calmly around your head, close in, one way.
Switch direction halfway through the set.
tips: Stand tall and still — it's the arms that circle, not the body.

kort: An explosive hip hinge: the bell swings to chest height on hip power — one arm at a time.
steg: Start with the bell slightly in front of you, grip with one hand.
Hike it back between your legs with a long spine.
Snap your hips forward — the bell floats up to chest height and falls back on its own. Switch arms each set.
alias: Single-arm kettlebell swing (switch arms each set)
tips: The arm is just a rope — the power comes from the hips.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top of each swing.

kort: From lying to standing with the weight overhead the whole way — control in slow motion.
steg: Lie on your back with the bell pressed to a straight arm, the same-side knee bent.
Roll up onto your elbow, then hand; lift your hip and thread the leg under to kneeling.
Stand up calmly with your eyes on the bell — and back down the same way.
tips: Do it first without weight or with a shoe balanced on your fist.
Take it calmly in each step — it's seven small exercises in one.

kort: Walk calmly with heavy dumbbells in your hands — pure everyday strength for grip, core and posture.
steg: Lift a heavy-enough dumbbell in each hand.
Walk calmly and tall for a set distance or time.
Set the weights down under control and rest.
tips: Shoulders down from your ears, gaze forward — like carrying grocery bags with style.

kort: Hold your body as a straight, stiff line on your forearms or straight arms.
steg: Place your elbows under your shoulders and stretch your legs out.
Brace your abs and glutes so your body becomes one straight line.
Hold while breathing calmly.
tips: Better a shorter hold with good form than a long one with sagging hips.

kort: A plank on your side: forearm on the floor, hips lifted — the side of the core holds you up.
steg: Lie on your side with your elbow under your shoulder.
Lift your hips until your body is a straight line.
Hold calmly, and switch sides.
alias: Side plank from knees or feet
tips: For an easier version: knees on the floor.

kort: McGill curl-up: hands under your lower back, lift your head and shoulders a touch — back-friendly core strength.
steg: Lie on your back, one knee bent, hands under the arch of your lower back.
Lift your head and shoulders a few centimeters without rounding your lower back.
Hold a few seconds, lower calmly. Switch legs halfway.
alias: Modified curl-up, hands under the lower back
tips: A tiny movement — the neck stays neutral.

kort: On all fours: extend the opposite arm and leg out, hold, and "draw a square" home — stability without load.
steg: Get on all fours with a straight back.
Extend one arm forward and the opposite leg back until they're in line with your body.
Hold a few seconds, return calmly and switch sides.
alias: Bird dog — draw a square with your hand on the way home
tips: Your hips should point straight down the whole time — no twisting.

kort: Lie on your back with your lower back pressed into the floor, shoulders and legs lifted — the gymnasts' core position.
steg: Lie on your back and press your lower back down into the floor.
Lift your shoulders and legs a little — start with bent knees.
Hold the position while breathing; extend the legs as you get stronger.
alias: Hollow hold (start with bent knees, extend gradually)
tips: If you lose floor contact in your lower back: bend your knees more.

kort: The hollow position in a calm rocking back and forth — the core keeps its shape.
steg: Get into the hollow hold position.
Rock calmly back and forth like a rocking chair.
The shape is sacred — if it disappears, take a break.

kort: Hold yourself on all fours with your knees lifted a touch off the floor — a stillness that burns.
steg: Get on all fours with your hands under your shoulders.
Lift your knees 2–3 cm off the floor.
Hold the position with calm breathing and your head in line with your spine.
alias: Bear position hold with the head up

kort: Up onto your toes and calmly down again — strengthens the calves and ankles.
steg: Stand with your feet hip-width apart, ideally with fingertip support on a wall.
Press up high onto your toes.
Lower calmly all the way down.
tips: A calm descent is half the exercise.

kort: A plank with your feet on the wall (belly toward the wall) — a safe path toward a handstand.
steg: Start in a plank with your feet against the wall.
Walk your feet up the wall and your hands closer — as far as is comfortable.
Hold briefly, and walk calmly down the same way.
alias: Wall plank / half wall walk, belly toward the wall
tips: Body in one line — no arching. Short and controlled wins.

kort: Calm kicks up to a handstand with the wall as safety.
steg: Place your hands a hand's length from the wall.
Kick up calmly with one leg — the other follows.
Let your heels find the wall, hold briefly, and come down under control.
alias: Optional: calm kick-ups to the wall, straight arms
tips: Straight arms the whole time — the shoulders actively push into the floor.

kort: Balance your knees against your upper arms with your weight forward — the first step toward crow pose.
steg: Place your hands on the floor shoulder-width apart, fingers spread.
Set your knees against your upper arms and lean your weight calmly forward.
Optionally lift one foot at a time — gaze slightly forward, not down.
alias: Crow prep (knees to upper arms, weight forward)
tips: Place a cushion in front of you the first few times.

kort: A single-leg hip hinge where the hip rotates in and out — the master exercise of balance.
steg: Stand on one leg and hinge your torso forward with a long spine.
Rotate the hip calmly out (the chest twists up) and then in.
Keep your fingertips on a wall if you like. Switch sides.
alias: Hip airplane from single-leg stance: rotate the hip in and out
tips: Small, calm rotations — it's the hip that steers.

kort: From kneeling to standing and back down — without using your hands.
steg: Kneel with a tall torso.
Set one foot forward and stand up without support.
Come calmly back down onto the opposite knee. Switch which leg goes first.
alias: Kneeling to standing transition without hands

kort: Controlled rotations with the neck through the full pain-free range of motion.
steg: Stand or sit tall, lower your shoulders.
Draw a large, slow circle with your nose: down, to the side, up and around.
Switch direction. Stay clear of anything that hurts.
tips: Slow is the point — the circle should take several breaths.

kort: Large, slow circles with a straight arm through the shoulder's full range of motion.
steg: Stand tall (or on all fours) with the rest of your body completely still.
Move one straight arm slowly forward, up, back and around in the largest possible circle.
Switch direction and arm.
alias: Shoulder CARs standing or on all fours
tips: The body is the statue — only the arm moves.

kort: Calm, full circles for the elbow and wrist — lubricates the joints where they can actually move.
steg: Keep your upper arm still and rotate the forearm slowly through its full range.
Then draw large circles with the wrist, both directions.
Switch arms.

kort: Segmented cat–cow: roll the spine slowly, vertebra by vertebra — control through the whole spinal column.
steg: Get on all fours.
Round your back slowly from tailbone to neck, vertebra by vertebra.
Arch back just as slowly the same way.
tips: Think of your spine as a bicycle chain — one link at a time.

kort: Large, slow circles with the knee: up, out to the side, back and home — the hip's full range.
steg: Stand with support from a wall or chair.
Lift one knee, bring it out to the side, rotate it back and set it down.
Reverse the direction. Switch sides.
tips: Keep the pelvis still — the circle happens in the hip joint.

kort: Calm circles and bend/extend for the knee and ankle through the full comfortable range.
steg: Stand on one leg with support, lift the other knee.
Draw small circles with the lower leg from the knee, both directions.
Finish with calm ankle circles. Switch sides.

kort: Small nods of the head up and down on all fours — wakes up deep neck control.
steg: Get on all fours with a long spine.
Nod your head calmly down (chin toward chest) and up, in a small range.
The rest of your back stays completely still.
alias: Head nods on all fours, up/down

kort: On all fours: turn your head calmly side to side like a "no" — neck rotation with support.
steg: Get on all fours with your neck in line with your spine.
Turn your head slowly to one side, back to center, then the other side.
Small range, no forcing.
alias: Head nod rotations side to side

kort: Roll from back to belly led by the arm — ancient, soft core coordination.
steg: Lie on your back with your arms overhead.
Reach one arm across your body and let it lead the roll onto your belly — the legs relax.
Roll back, and switch side/direction.
alias: Rolling back to belly led by the arm
tips: Slow and soft — the legs shouldn't kick you around.

kort: Rock calmly back and forth on all fours, heels toward your seat — gently opens the hips and knees.
steg: Get on all fours.
Push your hips calmly back toward your heels, as far as is comfortable.
Rock forward again and repeat in a calm rhythm.
alias: Rocking on all fours, heels toward the seat

kort: A small, soft jump forward from standing — landing with bent knees and full control.
steg: Stand with your feet hip-width apart.
Swing your arms and jump a short distance forward.
Land softly with bent knees, and walk calmly back.
tips: The landing is the exercise — silent and stable.

kort: Yoga's resting point: stand tall and still with your weight evenly distributed — everything starts here.
steg: Stand with your feet together or hip-width apart.
Lengthen tall through the crown of your head, let your shoulders drop.
Breathe calmly and feel your whole sole against the floor.

kort: The calm of mountain pose on a chair: sit tall with both feet on the floor.
steg: Sit on the edge of the chair with your feet flat on the floor.
Lengthen tall through the crown of your head and lower your shoulders.
Rest your hands on your thighs and breathe calmly.

kort: The inhale position of the sun salutation: the arms lift in a large arc overhead.
steg: From standing (or seated): lift your arms out and up on an inhale.
Lengthen tall without hiking your shoulders.
Your gaze can follow your hands if the neck wants to.
alias: Arms up with a straight back
Arms up with a straight back (inhale)

kort: The exhale position: fold your torso softly down over your legs with soft knees.
steg: Fold forward from your hips on an exhale.
Let your head and arms hang heavy — knees well bent if you like.
Rise calmly back up, vertebra by vertebra.
tips: Soft knees make the fold good for both back and hamstrings.

kort: Seated on a chair: fold your torso down over your thighs and let everything hang.
steg: Sit on the edge of the chair with your feet firmly on the floor.
Fold your torso down over your thighs on an exhale.
Let your head and arms hang heavy before you roll calmly up.

kort: Halfway up from the forward fold: a long, flat back and gaze forward.
steg: From the forward fold: lift your torso halfway on an inhale.
Hands on your shins or thighs, spine long as a table.
Release back down on an exhale.

kort: Yoga's low plank: lower yourself halfway with your elbows close to the body — knees down is perfectly fine.
steg: From a plank: lower yourself calmly halfway down.
The elbows point back and stay close to your ribs.
Set your knees down for control if you like.
tips: Think "lower with style" — knees over collapse.

kort: A soft backbend from the belly: the chest lifts with light help from the hands.
steg: Lie on your belly with your hands under your shoulders.
Lift your chest calmly on an inhale — the elbows stay bent.
The shoulders slide down from the ears. Lower on an exhale.
tips: Lift with your back, not your arms — the hands just assist.

kort: A powerful backbend on straight arms: hips and thighs lifted off the floor.
steg: From a low position: press up onto straight arms.
Roll over your toes so the tops of your feet are on the floor — thighs and hips lift.
The chest opens forward, shoulders down.
tips: For a gentler version: do cobra instead.

kort: An inverted V: hands and feet on the floor, hips high — the whole back of the body stretches.
steg: From all fours: lift your knees and push your hips up and back.
Push the floor away with straight arms — spine long.
Pedal calmly with bent knees if you like. The heels don't need to reach the floor.
tips: Bent knees and a long spine beat straight legs and a rounded back.

kort: Sit in an invisible chair: knees bent, chest lifted, arms forward or up.
steg: From standing: bend your knees and lower your hips as if toward a chair.
Weight in your heels, chest proud.
The arms reach forward or up. Hold and breathe.

kort: A lunge position with the hips forward and arms toward the ceiling — grounded and rising at once.
steg: Take a long step back and angle the back foot slightly out.
Bend the front knee over the ankle — the hips point forward.
Lift your arms toward the ceiling and lower your shoulders. Switch sides.

kort: A wide lunge with open hips: the arms reach out each way, gaze over the front hand.
steg: Stand wide; the front foot points forward, the back foot parallel to the back edge.
Bend the front knee over the ankle — the hips open to the side.
Reach your arms out each way and look over your front fingertips.

kort: From warrior II: the front arm lifts toward the ceiling while the back hand rests on the leg — a soft side arc.
steg: From warrior II: turn the front palm up.
Lift the arm up and back on an inhale — the back hand slides down the leg.
The front knee stays bent. Breathe in the arc, and switch sides.

kort: Straight legs, a long side bend over the front leg — the body opens like a triangular sail.
steg: From a wide stance: straighten the front leg and lean your torso forward over it.
Lower your hand to your shin or a block, the other arm toward the ceiling.
Open your chest and breathe. Switch sides.
tips: Better to lean high with an open torso than deep with a collapse.

kort: From warrior II: the forearm on the front thigh, the top arm reaching overhead — one long line.
steg: From warrior II: lower your forearm onto the front thigh (or your hand to the floor).
Reach the top arm overhead in line with your body.
Roll your chest up and breathe along the side line. Switch sides.

kort: Straight up and down: one arm overhead, lean calmly to the opposite side.
steg: Stand tall and lift one arm overhead.
Lean calmly to the opposite side without leaning forward or back.
Breathe in the stretch and switch sides.

kort: Seated: one arm overhead and lean calmly to the opposite side — the side body breathes.
steg: Sit tall (on a chair or the floor) with both sit bones heavy.
Lift one arm and lean calmly to the opposite side.
Hold a few breaths, come up and switch sides.

kort: A calm rotation from seated: turn your torso to one side and stay for a few breaths.
steg: Sit tall and place the opposite hand on your knee/thigh.
Grow tall on the inhale, twist calmly further on the exhale.
A few calm breaths — then switch sides.
tips: The rotation comes from above: the chest leads, the neck follows last.

kort: Cat–cow from a chair: round and arch your back alternately at the pace of your breath.
steg: Sit on the edge of the chair with your hands on your thighs.
Inhale: lift your chest and arch your back lightly.
Exhale: round your back and let your head drop softly. Repeat calmly.

kort: The spine's windshield wiper: alternate softly between a rounded and arched back on all fours, in time with your breath.
steg: Get on all fours with your hands under your shoulders.
Inhale: drop your belly, lift your chest and gaze (cow).
Exhale: round your back and let your head drop (cat). Repeat calmly.
tips: Let your breath set the pace — one breath per movement.

kort: Yoga's resting position: seat toward your heels, forehead to the mat, arms forward or along your sides.
steg: From kneeling: lower your seat toward your heels.
Rest your forehead on the mat and let your arms rest forward or back.
Breathe calmly into your back and stay as long as you like.
alias: Child's pose — forehead on the mat, arms along the sides
tips: Knees wide makes room for the belly; a cushion under the forehead makes everything better.

kort: Child's pose where your hands walk to one side — stretches the whole side line.
steg: Get into child's pose with your arms forward.
Walk both hands calmly over to one side until it stretches along the side.
A few calm breaths, then the hands walk over to the other side.

kort: The art of the ending: lie flat, let your body get heavy and do absolutely nothing.
steg: Lie on your back with your arms slightly out from your body, palms up.
Close your eyes and release all control of your breath.
Stay lying down — the stillness is the exercise itself.
alias: Savasana — flat on your back, let the body get heavy
tips: A blanket over the body makes it easier to let go.

navn: Roll to the side and sit up
kort: The gentle way out of savasana: roll onto your side, rest, and push yourself calmly up.
steg: Draw your knees softly in and roll onto one side.
Stay lying for a couple of breaths.
Push yourself calmly up to sitting with your hands.
alias: Roll to the side, sit up slowly

kort: Seated with the soles together: let your knees fall out and fold softly forward with a rounded back.
steg: Sit with the soles of your feet together, a little away from your hips.
Let your knees sink out to the sides under their own weight.
Fold softly forward with a rounded back and stay, breath by breath.
tips: In yin, a slack back is allowed — gravity does the work.

kort: The yin version of a seated forward fold: fold forward over straight legs and let your back round.
steg: Sit with your legs straight (slightly bent knees are fine).
Fold your torso softly forward and let your head hang.
Let your back round and stay in the fold for several calm breaths.

kort: Lying on your belly on your forearms: a mild, passive backbend you can rest in.
steg: Lie on your belly and place your elbows under your shoulders.
Let your forearms support you — chest open, shoulders down.
Release your seat and legs completely relaxed and stay in the position.
alias: Sphinx (lying on the belly on the forearms)

kort: Sphinx's big brother: press up onto straighter arms for a deeper, still passive backbend.
steg: From sphinx: place your hands on the floor and straighten your arms more.
Let your hips get heavy in the mat.
Come calmly down if your lower back speaks up.
tips: Sphinx is always a good alternative.

kort: The yin lunge: a long, low lunge position where the hip flexor gets plenty of time to release.
steg: From all fours: set one foot far forward between your hands.
Let the back knee rest on the floor and lower your hips forward and down.
Stay in the position for several breaths, and switch sides.
tips: A cushion under the back knee makes the dragon much friendlier.

kort: The yin version of pigeon: front shin across, back leg long behind — fold forward and stay.
steg: From all fours: bring one knee forward behind the wrist, the shin angled under you.
Stretch the other leg long behind you.
Fold your torso forward over the front leg and rest there. Switch sides.
tips: A cushion under the hip makes the position even and safe.

kort: Knees wide, hips back: a deep and demanding yin opener for the inner thighs.
steg: From all fours: push your knees wide out to the sides, shins on the floor.
Lower yourself onto your forearms and let your hips sink back.
Stay in the position with calm breaths — never go further than comfortable.
tips: Cushions under the knees are almost mandatory.

kort: A yin backbend from kneeling: lean back onto a cushion along your spine — the front of the thighs and hips open.
steg: Sit on your knees with a cushion/bolster behind you.
Lean calmly back onto the cushion as far as is comfortable.
Stay in the position and breathe — come calmly up onto your side.
alias: Saddle with a cushion along the spine
tips: If you feel your knee: try one side at a time or skip it.

kort: A mild inversion: the legs swing over the head with a folded blanket under the shoulders.
steg: Lie on your back with a folded blanket under your shoulders.
Swing your legs calmly over your head, supporting your back with your hands.
Stay only as long as it's comfortable — roll calmly back down.
alias: Snail with a folded blanket under the shoulders
tips: Never turn your head while in the position. Legs up the wall is a nice alternative.

kort: A lying banana: feet and torso travel to the same side — a long, lazy side stretch.
steg: Lie on your back and move your feet to one side.
Move your shoulders and arms to the same side — the body becomes a banana.
Relax completely and stay there. Switch sides.

kort: Kneeling with the chest melting toward the floor, arms reaching forward — opens the chest and shoulders.
steg: From all fours: keep your hips over your knees and walk your hands far forward.
Lower your chest toward the floor and let the heart "melt" down.
Your forehead or chin rests on the mat. Stay and breathe.
alias: Melting heart (anahatasana, kneeling, chest toward the floor)

kort: The back's favorite: the knees fall to one side while the shoulders rest on the floor.
steg: Lie on your back and draw your knees up.
Let them fall calmly to one side — arms out, shoulders heavy.
Look the opposite way if you like. A few calm breaths, and switch sides.
tips: Let your knees rest on a cushion if they don't reach the floor.

kort: A reclined twist with crossed legs — a deeper, more concentrated twist.
steg: Lie on your back and cross one leg over the other.
Let the crossed knees fall to one side, shoulders on the floor.
Stay in the twist for several breaths, and switch the cross and side.
alias: Twisted roots (reclined twist w/ bent, crossed knees)

navn: Side fold over a wide leg
kort: Seated with the legs wide: lean sideways over one leg and breathe into the side line.
steg: Sit with your legs wide apart.
Lean sideways down over one leg — your forearm can rest on the thigh.
The opposite arm reaches overhead. Switch sides.
alias: Side fold over a wide leg (side bend over a straight leg)

kort: The most restorative of them all: lie with your seat against the wall and your legs resting up.
steg: Sit sideways against a wall and swing your legs up as you lie down.
Seat as close to the wall as is comfortable — the arms rest open.
Stay in the position and let your breath settle.
alias: Viparita Karani — legs up the wall, seat against the wall
Viparita karani legs up the wall

kort: Reclined butterfly: soles of the feet together, knees open — rest with support and breathe.
steg: Lie on your back with the soles of your feet together.
Let your knees fall open — place cushions under them if you like.
Hands on your belly or out to the sides. Rest.
alias: Supta Baddha Konasana — soles together, knees out to the sides

kort: A calm stretch for the back of the thigh — choose a lying, seated or standing variant.
steg: Lie on your back and stretch one leg toward the ceiling (hold behind the thigh).
Draw the leg softly toward you until it stretches behind the thigh.
Breathe calmly, releasing a little more with each exhale. Switch sides.
alias: Hamstring lying (hands behind thigh, press the heel down)
tips: Never push into pain — 6–7 out of 10 in stretch feeling lasts a long time.

kort: A lying hamstring stretch with a band around the foot — precise and relaxed.
steg: Lie on your back with a band around the sole of your foot.
Extend the leg toward the ceiling and let the band hold it up.
Relax the rest of your body and breathe. Switch sides.

kort: A standing hamstring stretch: heel forward, hips leaning back.
steg: Set one heel slightly forward with a straight leg, toes up.
Lean your hips back with a long spine until it stretches behind the thigh.
Your hands can rest on the other thigh. Switch sides.
alias: Standing hamstring (heel forward, lean the hip back)

kort: From a lunge position: straighten the front leg and fold over it with a long spine.
steg: From a kneeling lunge: push your hips back and straighten the front leg.
Tip your toes toward you and fold your torso forward with a long spine.
Calm breaths, and switch sides.
alias: Half split (hamstring fold in a lunge)

kort: Calm work toward the splits with blocks or cushions as support — only as far as the body wants.
steg: From a half split: let the front foot slide further forward with support under your hands.
Lower yourself only as far as feels safe — cushions under the front thigh help.
Breathe calmly at the end range, and come up under control. Switch sides.
tips: This is patience work — millimeters are progress.

kort: A kneeling lunge: push your hip calmly forward until it stretches at the front of the back hip.
steg: Kneel in a lunge with the back knee on the floor.
Squeeze the back glute and push your hips calmly forward — without arching your back.
It stretches at the front of the hip. Breathe, and switch sides.
alias: Hip flexor in a deep lunge (press the back knee toward the floor)
Hip flexor in a deep lunge
tips: Lift the same-side arm for extra length.

kort: Side-lying: the heel is drawn toward the seat while the hip is actively extended back.
steg: Lie on your side and grab the ankle of the top leg.
Draw the heel toward your seat and press the hip actively forward.
Keep your ribs down. Switch sides.
alias: Side-lying hip flexor (heel to seat, active hip extension)

kort: A classic front-of-thigh stretch: heel to the seat, knees together.
steg: Stand on one leg (hold onto something if needed) and grab the opposite ankle.
Draw the heel softly toward your seat with the knees close together.
Stand tall — don't arch. Switch sides.
alias: Stretch the front of the thigh

kort: A stretch for the calf: the back leg straight with the heel on the floor, lean toward the wall.
steg: Stand facing a wall with one leg well back.
Press the back heel into the floor and lean forward until it stretches in the calf.
Switch sides. Bend the back knee slightly to reach deeper into the calf.
alias: Calf against the wall (press the ball of the foot to the wall)

kort: A seated calf stretch: band around the sole of the foot, pull your toes toward you.
steg: Sit with one leg straight and a band around the sole of your foot.
Pull your toes calmly toward you until it stretches in the calf.
Hold and breathe. Switch sides.

kort: Lying down: the leg is guided out to the side with a band — the inner thigh releases calmly.
steg: Lie on your back with a band around one sole of the foot.
Extend the leg up and let it fall calmly out to the side.
The opposite hip stays heavy on the floor. Switch sides.
alias: Lying adductor w/ band (leg out to the side)

kort: Seated against a wall with the knees open: press your knees lightly against your hands — an active inner-thigh stretch.
steg: Sit with your back against a wall and the soles of your feet together.
Place your hands on the inside of your knees.
Press your knees lightly against your hands for a few seconds, release — and let them sink a little deeper.
alias: Seated adductor at the wall (press the knees against the hands)

kort: Open the chest: arms behind the body or against a doorframe — the antidote to a day at the screen.
steg: Interlace your hands behind your back (or hold onto a doorframe).
Lift your chest and draw your shoulders softly back.
Breathe into your chest and hold.

kort: Side-lying: the top arm opens in a large arc out and down — the chest follows.
steg: Lie on your side with your knees bent and your arms together in front of you.
Lift the top arm and sweep it in a large arc over to the other side.
Let your gaze follow your hand, and sweep calmly back. Switch sides.

kort: A side-lying rotation: the top arm opens like a page of a book — hold at the end range.
steg: Lie on your side with your knees bent 90 degrees and your arms reaching in front of you.
Open the top arm over to the opposite side, like a book being opened.
Hold at the end range for a few breaths — the knees stay put. Switch sides.
alias: Open book (thoracic rotation, hold the end range)

kort: Pull one arm across your chest — a simple and effective shoulder stretch.
steg: Bring one arm straight across your chest.
Use the other arm to draw it softly closer.
Keep the shoulder low. Switch sides.

kort: Hands on the wall, chest lowering down — the shoulders open into a full stretch.
steg: Place your hands on the wall at shoulder height and step back a little.
Lower your chest down between your arms with a long spine.
Breathe where it stretches in your shoulders and chest.

kort: Let your ear sink calmly toward your shoulder — the neck's simplest and best stretch.
steg: Sit or stand tall and lower both shoulders.
Let one ear sink calmly toward that shoulder.
A hand can rest lightly on top of the head — weight only, never pulling. Switch sides.

kort: Stretch the forearms: straight arm, bend the hand up and down with light help.
steg: Stretch one arm forward with the palm up.
Bend the hand down with light help from the other — it stretches in the forearm.
Turn the palm down and repeat the other way. Switch arms.

kort: A deep side stretch for the lower back's side muscle: lean over with a raised arm and let the side breathe.
steg: Sit or stand with good height.
Lift one arm and lean calmly over to the opposite side, slightly forward.
Breathe into the side of your back. Switch sides.

kort: A lunge, elbow to the floor and rotation toward the ceiling — hips, back and chest in one movement.
steg: Take a long lunge and place both hands inside the front foot.
Lower the elbow on the same side as the front leg toward the floor.
Then rotate that arm up toward the ceiling and follow with your gaze. Switch sides.
alias: World's greatest stretch: lunge, elbow to the floor, rotation, hip back
tips: Take the movement slowly — it isn't called "world's greatest" for nothing.

kort: The ankle over the opposite knee — the glutes and deep hip rotators let go.
steg: Lying down: place one ankle over the opposite knee.
Grab behind the thigh and draw the legs softly toward you.
Seated on a chair: lean calmly forward instead. Switch sides.

kort: Both legs at 90 degrees on the floor: lean over the front shin with a long spine.
steg: Sit with the front leg bent 90 degrees in front of you and the back leg 90 degrees to the side.
Grow tall and lean your torso calmly forward over the front shin.
Breathe where it stretches in the glute. Switch sides.
alias: 90/90 hip stretch (lean over the front shin)
Leaning forward over the front shin with a long spine

kort: From 90/90: the knees swing together over to the opposite side without hands — active hip control.
steg: Sit in the 90/90 position with your hands lightly behind you (or lifted).
Swing both knees calmly over to the opposite side.
Control the whole way — no speed. Repeat side to side.
alias: 90/90 tip side to side with the hands behind

kort: From 90/90: lift the back foot off the floor while the knee stays down — strength in the hip's internal rotation.
steg: Sit in 90/90 with a tall torso.
Lift the back foot off the floor without the knee leaving the ground.
Hold a couple of seconds, lower calmly. Repeat and switch sides.
alias: Lift-off internal rotation: back foot lifts, knee on the ground
tips: Small movement, big effect — don't lean away.

kort: From 90/90: lift the entire front shin off the floor — the strength test of external rotation.
steg: Sit in 90/90 with your hands lightly behind you.
Lift the entire front shin a few centimeters off the floor.
Hold briefly, lower calmly. Repeat and switch sides.
alias: Lift-off external rotation: front shin lifts off the floor

kort: From pigeon pose: press your torso up and down from the front shin — an active hip opener.
steg: Get into pigeon pose with the front shin on the floor.
Press your hands into the floor and lift your torso — feel the pressure in the front hip.
Lower calmly back down. Repeat and switch sides.
alias: Pigeon push-up: press up from the front shin

kort: Sit or lie still and let your breath become long and low — the nervous system's off switch.
steg: Find a comfortable position, seated or lying down.
Breathe in through your nose, low down into your belly.
Let the exhale become a little longer than the inhale. Continue calmly.

kort: Lying with your hands on your belly: breathe so your hands rise — deep, calm diaphragmatic breathing.
steg: Lie on your back with bent knees and your hands on your belly.
Breathe in through your nose so your belly (and hands) rise.
Breathe slowly out and feel your belly sink. Repeat calmly.
alias: Belly breathing lying on the back

kort: Breathe in a square: in 4 — hold 4 — out 4 — hold 4. Simple, powerful, calming.
steg: Breathe in through your nose while counting to 4.
Hold your breath for 4. Breathe out for 4. Hold empty for 4.
Repeat the rounds at a calm pace.
tips: If 4 is too long: use 3. The rhythm matters more than the number.

kort: About 5.5 breaths per minute: equal inhale and exhale — heart calm on pure rhythm.
steg: Breathe in calmly for about 5.5 seconds.
Breathe out just as calmly for about 5.5 seconds.
Continue — smooth as a wave, without pauses.

kort: A double inhale through the nose, a long exhale through the mouth — the fastest way to a calmer heart rate.
steg: Breathe in through your nose, and take a small extra inhale at the top.
Release a long, relaxed exhale through your mouth.
Repeat for one to two minutes.

kort: The gentlest pressing movement: stand against a wall and push in and out with a straight body — learn the direction without loading the joints.
steg: Stand an arm's length from the wall with your feet hip-width apart and your hands flat on the wall at chest height, a little wider than your shoulders.
Brace your abs and glutes so your body is one straight line from head to heel.
Bend your elbows calmly and let your chest come toward the wall — the elbows point diagonally back, not straight out to the sides.
Press yourself back to straight arms without losing the straight line.
tips: Want it harder? Move your feet farther from the wall.
The hips don't lag behind and don't stick up — the whole body moves as one plank.

kort: Sit calmly down toward a chair and stand up without your hands — the safest entry to the squat.
steg: Stand in front of a chair with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly out.
Push your hips back and sit calmly down until your seat just touches the chair.
Keep your weight on your heels and your chest proud.
Stand up without pushing off with your hands, if you can.
tips: The slower you lower yourself, the more strength you build.
Managing it easily? Use a lower chair, or drop the touch and switch to a free squat.

kort: The easiest pulling movement: stand fairly upright and pull your chest toward your hands — the higher the angle, the easier.
steg: Grip rings, a bar or the edge of a sturdy table at chest height.
Walk your feet slightly forward so you lean back with a straight body — fairly upright to begin with.
Pull your chest toward your hands by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Lower yourself calmly back to straight arms with full control.
tips: More upright = easier. Want it harder, walk your feet farther forward so your body is more horizontal.
Keep your body straight as a plank the whole way — no sagging in the lower back.

kort: Lie on your back and press your hips toward the ceiling — the simplest way to wake up and strengthen the glutes.
steg: Lie on your back with bent knees and your feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Press through your heels and lift your hips until your body is a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Squeeze your glutes hard for one second at the top.
Lower your hips calmly back down without fully releasing the tension.
tips: The power comes from the glutes, not the lower back — don't arch your back at the top.
Feel it mostly in your hamstrings? Move your feet a little closer to your seat.

kort: Push-ups with your hands raised on a sofa or bench — the lower the surface, the harder. The bridge between wall and floor.
steg: Place your hands on the edge of a sofa or bench, a little wider than your shoulders.
Walk your feet back until your body is a straight, tight line from head to heel.
Lower your chest calmly toward the edge with your elbows about 45 degrees in toward the body.
Press yourself back up to straight arms without losing the plank.
tips: Lower the surface height when it feels easy — a stair step, sofa, low bench.
The hips follow the body — no sag and no peak.

kort: A free squat without support: sit down between your hips until your thighs are horizontal, and stand up.
steg: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly out.
Push your hips back and down as if toward an invisible chair — the knees follow the direction of the toes.
Go down until your thighs are roughly horizontal, with your heels on the floor and chest up.
Press your whole foot into the floor and stand all the way up.
tips: Weight evenly on the whole foot with good contact in the heels.
Knees hurting? Check that they point the same way as the toes, and don't go deeper than you have control.

kort: The same pull as high angle, but your body is more horizontal now — a real step up in back strength.
steg: Grip rings or a bar at chest height and walk your feet forward until your body is well angled.
Brace your abs and glutes so your body is a straight line.
Pull your chest toward your hands with your shoulders down and back and your elbows close to the body.
Lower yourself calmly out to straight arms with full control.
tips: Pull with your back, not your arms — think "elbows back and shoulder blades together".
The more horizontal your body, the harder the pull.

kort: A glute bridge on one leg — twice the work on the glute, and it reveals if one side is weaker.
steg: Lie on your back with bent knees and your feet on the floor.
Extend one leg straight out, in line with the thigh.
Press through the heel of the leg on the floor and lift your hips to a straight line.
Squeeze the glute at the top, lower calmly, and finish the set before you switch sides.
tips: Keep your hips level — don't let the free side sink down.
Too easy? Hold a short pause at the top of each lift.

kort: Lie on your back and lower the opposite arm and leg calmly while your lower back presses into the floor — safe, deep core strength.
steg: Lie on your back with your arms straight up and your hips and knees bent 90 degrees.
Press your lower back down into the floor so the arch disappears.
Lower the opposite arm and leg calmly toward the floor without your back lifting.
Return to start and switch sides at a calm pace.
tips: Losing floor contact in your lower back? Lower less, or keep the legs still and move only the arms.
Breathe out calmly as you extend — it helps the core hold.

kort: Push-ups on the knees — the same movement with less load, the direct bridge to a full push-up from the toes.
steg: Get on all fours and set your hands a little wider than your shoulders, slightly in front of them.
Lower your knees to the floor and stretch your body out to a straight line from knees to head.
Lower your chest calmly toward the floor with your elbows about 45 degrees in.
Press back up without your hips sinking or sticking up.
tips: Put something soft under your knees if it pinches.
Ready for more? Move your hands farther forward, or finish with one rep from the toes.

kort: A squat with weight held to the chest — the weight balances you and makes it easier to sit deep and upright.
steg: Hold a dumbbell, kettlebell or something heavy with both hands against your chest.
Sit calmly down between your hips with your heels on the floor.
Go as deep as you have control, with your elbows inside your knees at the bottom.
Press your whole foot into the floor and stand up with your chest proud.
tips: The weight in front helps you keep your torso upright.
Let your elbows slide inside your knees at the bottom — that opens the hips.

kort: Upper back against the edge of the sofa, press your hips up to horizontal — the strongest posterior-chain exercise you can do at home.
steg: Sit on the floor with the upper part of your back against the edge of a sofa, feet flat on the floor.
Press through your heels and lift your hips until your body is horizontal from knees to shoulders.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and keep your chin lightly tucked.
Lower your hips calmly and repeat without fully resting at the bottom.
tips: Your shins should be roughly vertical at the top — adjust your foot placement until it matches.
Want more? Hold a pause at the top, or place a weight over your hips.

kort: Lie on your back, press your lower back down and lift your shoulders and bent legs — the foundation of all core strength.
steg: Lie on your back and press your lower back hard down into the floor.
Lift your shoulders a little off the floor and draw your knees up toward 90 degrees.
Hold the tension with bent legs (tuck) while breathing calmly.
Extend your legs further out as you get stronger — but only as long as your lower back stays on the floor.
tips: Losing floor contact in your lower back? Draw your knees closer to your chest again.
A small, steady position beats a large one that collapses.

kort: A step backward and lower the back knee toward the floor — safer for the knees and easier to balance than a forward lunge.
steg: Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart.
Take a controlled step backward and lower the back knee calmly toward the floor.
Keep your weight on the front leg with the heel on the floor and your torso tall.
Push off the front foot back to standing, and switch sides.
tips: A shorter step gives more in the thighs, a longer step more in the glutes.
Wobbling? Keep a fingertip against a wall for balance.

kort: Steer your effort with your breath: the talk test shows whether you're in the easy, moderate or hard zone — completely without a heart rate monitor.
steg: Notice your breath as you move: if you can talk in full sentences, you're in the easy zone.
If speech only comes in short phrases, you're in the moderate zone. If you can manage only single words, it's hard.
Adjust your pace up or down until your breath matches the zone the session calls for.
tips: RPE 1–10 is the same idea: 3–4 is easy, 5–7 moderate, 8–9 hard.

kort: Good walking: stand tall, roll the foot from heel to toe and let your arms swing relaxed in time with your steps.
steg: Stand tall with your gaze forward and your shoulders lowered and relaxed.
Set the heel down first and roll softly through the whole foot to the toe-off.
Let your arms swing naturally from the shoulder — opposite arm to opposite leg.
tips: Short, frequent steps carry you better than long, dragging steps.

kort: Walking in a comfortable zone 1 where you can talk and sing. The base session that builds endurance and brings you back to recovery.
steg: Walk at a pace where your breathing is completely free and you can talk effortlessly.
Keep your shoulders loose and your breath even — this should feel easy.
Use easy walking as a base session, warm-up and rest between harder efforts.
tips: Does it feel "too easy"? Then you're right on target in zone 1.

kort: Brisk, purposeful walking in zone 2–3: breathe noticeably harder, active arms and long, powerful steps — but you keep it going.
steg: Increase your pace until your breathing becomes clearly heavier, but you can still get out short sentences.
Bend your elbows around 90° and pump your arms actively to drive the pace up.
Take longer, more powerful push-offs and keep your torso tall.
tips: Aim for around 100–120 steps per minute for good brisk walking.

kort: Walk uphill safely and strongly: lean slightly forward from the ankles, shorten your steps and let the glutes and calves do the work.
steg: Lean your whole body slightly forward from the ankles — don't bend at the hip.
Shorten your steps and increase your cadence instead of taking long strides.
Push off with the whole foot and use your arms actively uphill.
tips: The hill sets the intensity — keep an even breath and lower the pace as needed.

kort: Run light and injury-free: tall posture, a quick and light cadence, and let the foot land under your body — not far in front.
steg: Run tall and relaxed with your gaze forward; lean slightly forward from the ankles.
Take short, quick steps — aim for around 170–180 steps per minute.
Let your foot land under your hips, not far in front, and keep your shoulders and hands loose.
tips: Think "light, silent steps" — less braking means less load.

kort: Alternate between short jogging intervals and walking breaks. The safest start to running — the body tolerates more when you break it up.
steg: Warm up with a few minutes of brisk walking.
Jog calmly for a set interval (e.g. one minute), then walk until you're recovered.
Repeat the alternation, and increase the jogging time a little from week to week.
tips: Keep the jogging efforts so calm that the walking break almost feels unnecessary — then the pace is right.

kort: An easy jog in zone 2 at a conversational pace the whole way. It almost feels too easy, but this is where the endurance engine is built.
steg: Jog at a pace where you can hold a conversation in full sentences.
Keep your steps light and your breath even — drop the pace chase.
Slow down rather than blow past the zone; easy should be easy.
tips: Most of your sessions should be here — roughly 80% easy, 20% hard.

kort: A "comfortably hard" pace in zone 3–4 you can hold for quite a while: heavy breathing, short phrases — the edge where the body tolerates the most speed.
steg: Find the pace that is comfortably hard — you can get out a few words, not full sentences.
Hold it steady through the whole effort without tipping over into all-out.
Break it into intervals with short breaks to stay in the right zone.
tips: Start too hard and the pace falls off — better to sit just under and hold it steady.

kort: Short, hard efforts in zone 4–5 with breaks in between: this is where top speed and fitness rise fastest — but dose the hard work wisely.
steg: Warm up well before the first effort — hard work requires a warm body.
Run the effort hard and steady as prescribed, not all out in the first seconds.
Take the prescribed break (easy jog or walk) so you're ready for the next effort.
tips: Quality over quantity: stop while the efforts still hold their pace.

kort: The core of HIIT: alternate between short, hard work efforts and breaks. This lets you work harder than you could hold continuously.
steg: Choose a work effort (e.g. 20–60 seconds) where you really push, and a break afterward.
In the work you give near-maximal effort — your breathing should get heavy and speech break up.
Use the break to recover a little, and repeat. It's the alternation that gives the effect.
tips: The body burns extra for a while after the session (EPOC) — that's why HIIT is so time-efficient.

kort: 20 seconds of maximal work, 10 seconds of rest, 8 rounds = 4 brutally hard minutes. The most famous HIIT recipe.
steg: Choose one exercise you can do safely at high speed.
Work all out for 20 seconds, rest for 10 — that's one round.
Repeat for 8 rounds total. Four minutes, but they should sting.
tips: The original (Tabata, 1996) was done on a bike at very high intensity — start easier until your fitness is dialed in.

kort: One minute split into 30 sec easy, 20 sec moderate and 10 sec max — repeated. A smart, self-regulating way to dose speed.
steg: Move easily for 30 seconds, increase to moderate for 20, and give it all for the last 10.
That makes one minute. Repeat five times, then take a break.
The short max part raises the intensity without making the whole session impossibly hard.
tips: Developed at the University of Copenhagen (Gunnarsson & Bangsbo) — improves fitness on a low training volume.

kort: Run in place with your knees high toward hip height at a quick pace. A simple, effective heart-rate driver with no equipment at all.
steg: Run in place and lift your knees alternately toward hip height.
Land softly on the forefoot and use your arms actively for pace.
Keep your torso tall and your abs lightly braced.
tips: Want it easier? Walk with high knees instead of running.

kort: A jumping straddle: jump your feet out to the sides while your arms go up overhead, and gather again. A classic full-body cardio exercise.
steg: Jump your feet out to the sides while lifting your arms out and up overhead.
Jump back and lower your arms in one smooth motion.
Keep a steady, quick rhythm and land softly with slightly bent knees.
tips: No jumping: step-jacks — step one leg out to the side at a time.

kort: An explosive squat: lower down and jump powerfully up, land softly with bent knees. Builds explosive leg power and heart rate.
steg: Lower yourself into a squat with your chest lifted.
Shoot explosively up into a jump from the whole foot.
Land softly with bent knees and go straight down into the next.
tips: No jumping: do quick, deep squats instead — nearly as effective.

kort: Jump and switch legs in the air from lunge to lunge. An explosive single-leg exercise that challenges heart rate, power and balance.
steg: Start in a lunge with both knees bent.
Jump straight up and switch your legs in the air, so the opposite leg is forward.
Land softly in a new lunge and continue in rhythm.
tips: Too much? Do alternating lunges without the jump — step forward and back at an easy pace.

kort: The king of HIIT: from standing down with your hands, jump your legs back to a plank, jump in again and up in a hop. The whole body.
steg: Place your hands on the floor and jump (or step) your legs back to a plank.
Take a push-up if you like, then jump your legs back in toward your hands.
Stand up in a jump with your arms overhead, and repeat.
tips: Easier: drop the jump and the push-up — step out and in and stand up calmly.

kort: Mountain climbers: in a plank you draw your knees alternately and quickly in toward your chest. Trains the core and heart rate at once.
steg: Start in a steady plank on your hands with a straight body.
Draw one knee quickly in toward your chest, and switch legs at a running pace.
Keep your hips low and your abs braced — don't let your seat bounce up.
tips: Take it easier by stepping the knees in one at a time instead of running.

kort: Steer your effort by zones (Z1–Z5) from heart rate or power. Your FTP sets the boundaries — the foundation of all smart bike training.
steg: Divide your effort into zones: Z1–Z2 is easy, Z3–Z4 is hard but steady, Z5 is max.
With a power meter, the zones are set from FTP (threshold power); otherwise you steer by heart rate and feel.
Put most of your rides in the low zones and the few hard ones at the top — a polarized distribution.
tips: No meter? The talk test works: easy = talk freely, hard = short phrases, max = single words.

kort: Steady, comfortable cycling in zone 1–2 where you can talk. The bedrock of all endurance — this is where the big aerobic engine is built.
steg: Hold a steady, comfortable pace where your breathing is free and you can talk.
Aim for a round, even cadence around 85–95 rpm.
Use the ride to build volume — time in the saddle counts more than speed.
tips: Does it feel "too easy"? Then you're right in zone 2 — most people ride their base rides too hard.

kort: Very light pedaling (soft-pedal) that keeps the legs moving without load. Active rest between efforts and on easy days.
steg: Pedal completely lightly in zone 1 with low resistance — your legs should barely feel it.
Use it as an active break between hard intervals to recover.
A full recovery session should feel too easy — the point is to move blood, not to train.
tips: Tempted to push? Don't — recovery only works when it's genuinely easy.

kort: Cadence: how many revolutions you pedal per minute (rpm). Round, even pedaling technique and the right cadence spare the legs and give speed.
steg: Pedal round and even — think of "scraping mud off your shoe" at the bottom of the stroke.
Find a comfortable base cadence around 85–95 rpm on flat ground.
Practice spin-ups: raise the cadence smoothly to 110–120+ rpm in an easy gear without bouncing in the saddle.
tips: Bouncing in the saddle at high cadence? Ease off a little and build the smoothness up gradually.

kort: Cycling uphill or in a heavy gear with lower cadence and more force. Alternate seated and standing to spread the load.
steg: On a climb: choose a gear that lets you hold steady force without grinding to a stall.
Sit and pedal with a calm, powerful cadence (60–75 rpm) and a steady torso.
Rise up when it gets steep or your legs need a change — rock the bike lightly.
tips: Standing pedaling gives more power but costs more — use it in doses.

kort: A "comfortably hard" pace around threshold (sweet spot / Z3–Z4) you can hold for a long time. Moves the limit for how long you can sustain speed.
steg: Find the pace that is hard but steady — around 88–95% of threshold (FTP).
Keep the load stable through the whole effort, not jerky.
Break it into blocks of 8–20 minutes with short breaks.
tips: Sweet spot (just below threshold) gives a lot of effect for the wear — the backbone of many plans.

kort: Short, hard efforts in zone 5 (3–5 min, or 30/15) that raise maximal oxygen uptake fastest. Dose the hard work wisely.
steg: Warm up well, then do hard efforts where your breathing maxes out — 3–5 min or short 30/15 efforts.
Hold a steady, high load through the effort instead of emptying yourself at the start.
Take the prescribed break (light pedaling) so you can hold the quality in the next effort.
tips: One to two such sessions a week is enough — they're very demanding and need recovery.
